segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: Imagine being the last of your kind. Not the last in a herd. Not the last in a valley.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: Why is the years important?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: The last. That's a heavy thought. And the strange part is... you would never know. Thousands of years ago, mammoths roamed enormous stretches of Earth.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: They crossed frozen plains. Survived brutal winters. And lived in a world of ice. A world that wasn't going to stay frozen.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: Slowly, the climate changed. The ice retreated. Forests spread. Grasslands disappeared. The mammoths' home began to shrink.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: Not overnight. Generation by generation. Exactly.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: The change was so slow that no mammoth could notice it happening. Yet every generation inherited a slightly smaller world.

segments.speaker: Reflector
segments.text: That's almost worse than a sudden disaster. Perhaps. Because there was no single moment to point to.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: No obvious ending. Just fewer mammoths. Then fewer still. Until only small groups remained. And eventually...

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: one final group. Imagine standing on an Arctic island. Cold wind. Endless sea. And somewhere out there walks one of the last mammoths.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: How did the mammoths...?

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: Looking for food. Following familiar paths. Living an ordinary day. Completely unaware that history is ending around it.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: No animal knows it's the last. No ceremony marks the moment. No witness records it. One day the mammoths were here.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: Then they weren't. The world kept moving. The oceans continued rising and falling. The seasons continued changing.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: But something ancient had vanished.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: A creature that had walked the Earth for hundreds of thousands of years. Gone. That's the unsettling thing about extinction.

segments.speaker: Narrator
segments.text: The planet doesn't stop. Life simply turns the page. And yet... thousands of years later... we still remember them.

segments.speaker: Reflector
segments.text: Not because they survived. Because their story did.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text:
I liked the story.
I didn't understand all of it.
But I think I understood the end.

segments.speaker: Think
segments.text: I'll remember the last for a long time.
